tagline.tech: Spiky-haired energy warrior. Powers up with every concept mastered.
tagline.nontech: A hero who gets stronger every time you learn something new.

tagline.tech: Red-capped jumper. Leaps from stage to stage across the map.
tagline.nontech: A bouncy adventurer who hops across the world collecting knowledge.

modes.label: Tech
modes.blurb: Precise framing for builders and the curious-technical.

prompt.tech: A Large Language Model (LLM) is best described as…
prompt.nontech: An AI chatbot like ChatGPT mostly works by…

text.tech: A statistical model that predicts the next token from patterns it learned during training.
text.nontech: Guessing the next words — like a super-powered autocomplete.

text.tech: A hand-written database of rules an engineer typed out.
text.nontech: Looking up answers in a fixed list someone wrote by hand.

text.tech: A live search engine querying the open web in real time.
text.nontech: Secretly searching Google for you every time.

correct.tech: Right — an LLM generates likely tokens from learned weights. No live lookup, no actions on its own.
correct.nontech: Yes! It predicts words. On its own it doesn't "know" facts or "do" things — it just writes the next likely words.

wrong.tech: Not quite — an LLM generates from learned weights; it doesn't query a database or the web by itself.
wrong.nontech: Close, but it's really just predicting words from everything it read while training.

recap.tech: LLM = a next-token predictor trained on huge text.
recap.nontech: An LLM is a word-prediction engine — powerful autocomplete.

title.nontech: What an LLM can't do alone

prompt.tech: Flip each card. Myth on front — the reality is on the back.
prompt.nontech: Tap each card to flip it and see the truth.

back.tech: It can sound confident yet be wrong — it predicts plausible text, not verified facts ("hallucination").
back.nontech: It can sound sure and still be wrong. It guesses words that *sound* right.

back.tech: By default it only sees what's in the current context window — no long-term memory unless you add one.
back.nontech: By itself it forgets once the chat ends, unless something is built to help it remember.

front.tech: "An LLM can take actions for me."
front.nontech: "AI can book my flight by itself."

back.tech: A raw LLM only outputs text. It needs tools wired up before it can *do* anything in the world.
back.nontech: On its own it can only talk. To actually *do* things it needs extra tools connected to it.

correct.tech: Exactly — these three limits are why we wrap LLMs with tools, memory, and checks.
correct.nontech: Got it — an LLM is smart with words, but it needs help to be right, remember, and act.

wrong.tech: Flip all the cards to continue.
wrong.nontech: Flip all the cards to continue.

recap.tech: Raw LLMs can hallucinate, lack memory, and can't act.
recap.nontech: An LLM can be wrong, forgets, and can't do things by itself.

prompt.tech: Drag each part into the agent frame. An agent = LLM + Goal + Tools + Memory, wired in a loop.
prompt.nontech: Give your helper everything it needs: a brain, a job, some tools, and a notepad to remember.

correct.tech: That's an agent: an LLM that can plan toward a goal, call tools, and keep state.
correct.nontech: You built it! A helper with a brain, a job, tools, and a memory can actually get things done.

wrong.tech: Not quite — each part has its matching slot. Try again.
wrong.nontech: Almost — each piece has its own spot. Give it another go.

recap.tech: Agent = LLM + goal + tools + memory in a loop.
recap.nontech: An agent is an LLM given a job, tools, and a memory.

title.nontech: How a helper gets work done

prompt.tech: Put the agent's loop in order. It repeats these until the goal is met.
prompt.nontech: Drag these into the right order — the helper repeats them until the job is done.

correct.tech: That's the loop: plan → act → observe → repeat. It's what makes an agent autonomous.
correct.nontech: Perfect — decide, do, check, repeat. That cycle is what lets it work on its own.

wrong.tech: Not the right order — think plan, then act, then observe. Try again.
wrong.nontech: Not quite the right order — decide first, then do, then check. Try again.

recap.tech: Agents run a plan→act→observe loop until done.
recap.nontech: A helper decides, does, checks, and repeats.

prompt.tech: Drop each capability under the right system.
prompt.nontech: Which one just talks, and which one actually does things for you?

label.nontech: Works through a to-do list for you

correct.tech: Spot on — the LLM is the brain; the agent is the brain plus hands, tools, and a loop.
correct.nontech: Exactly — an LLM talks; an agent uses that talking to actually get things done.

wrong.tech: A couple are misplaced — remember: agents act, LLMs generate. Try again.
wrong.nontech: A couple are in the wrong column — "does things" vs "just talks." Try again.

recap.tech: LLM generates; an agent acts using an LLM + tools + loop.
recap.nontech: LLM = talks. Agent = uses talking to actually do things.

prompt.tech: Match each real-world task to whether a plain LLM or a full agent fits best.
prompt.nontech: Sort each job: does it just need writing, or does something need to get done?

label.tech: Research a topic across the web + compile
label.nontech: Research online and build a report

label.tech: Book travel and update the calendar
label.nontech: Book a trip and add it to your calendar

label.tech: Rewrite text in a friendlier tone

correct.tech: Nice — one-shot text → LLM; multi-step work that touches tools/systems → agent.
correct.nontech: Great — if it's just writing, an LLM is plenty. If something must actually happen, you want an agent.

wrong.tech: A couple are misplaced — if a task needs tools or many steps, it's agent territory. Try again.
wrong.nontech: A couple are in the wrong column — "just writing" vs "get it done." Try again.

recap.tech: One-shot text → LLM; multi-step, tool-using work → agent.
recap.nontech: Writing? Use an LLM. Getting things done? Use an agent.
